title: Pythagorean Identity
content: sin^2(x) + cos^2(x) = 1 for all real x. This is the fundamental trigonometric identity derived from the unit circle definition. Every point (cos x, sin x) lies on the unit circle x^2 + y^2 = 1.

title: Secondary Pythagorean Identities
content: Dividing sin^2(x) + cos^2(x) = 1 by cos^2(x) gives 1 + tan^2(x) = sec^2(x). Dividing by sin^2(x) gives 1 + cot^2(x) = csc^2(x). These are essential for simplifying expressions involving tan, sec, cot, and csc.

title: Reciprocal Identities
content: The six trigonometric functions form three reciprocal pairs: csc(x) = 1/sin(x), sec(x) = 1/cos(x), and cot(x) = 1/tan(x). These hold wherever the denominator is non-zero.
formula_latex: \csc x = \frac{1}{\sin x}, \quad \sec x = \frac{1}{\cos x}, \quad \cot x = \frac{1}{\tan x}
example: If sin(x) = 1/2, then csc(x) = 2. If cos(x) = sqrt(3)/2, then sec(x) = 2/sqrt(3) = 2*sqrt(3)/3.

title: Quotient Identities
content: tan(x) = sin(x)/cos(x) and cot(x) = cos(x)/sin(x). These relate the tangent and cotangent functions to sine and cosine, and are used extensively to convert expressions into a single function type for simplification.
formula_latex: \tan x = \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}, \quad \cot x = \frac{\cos x}{\sin x}

title: Sine of Sum and Difference
content: sin(A + B) = sin A cos B + cos A sin B and sin(A - B) = sin A cos B - cos A sin B. These compound angle formulas are foundational and used to derive double-angle, half-angle, and product formulas.

title: Cosine of Sum and Difference
content: cos(A + B) = cos A cos B - sin A sin B and cos(A - B) = cos A cos B + sin A sin B. Note the sign reversal compared to the sine formula: the sum formula has a minus sign.

title: Tangent of Sum and Difference
content: tan(A + B) = (tan A + tan B)/(1 - tan A tan B) and tan(A - B) = (tan A - tan B)/(1 + tan A tan B). These are undefined when the denominator equals zero, which occurs when A + B or A - B equals an odd multiple of pi/2.

title: Double Angle Formula for Sine
content: sin(2A) = 2 sin A cos A. This is derived by setting B = A in the sin(A + B) formula. It is widely used in integration, solving equations, and simplifying expressions.

title: Double Angle Formulas for Cosine
content: cos(2A) has three equivalent forms: cos^2 A - sin^2 A, 2cos^2 A - 1, and 1 - 2sin^2 A. The choice of form depends on the context. The last two are especially useful for deriving half-angle formulas and power-reduction identities.

title: Double Angle Formula for Tangent
content: tan(2A) = 2 tan A / (1 - tan^2 A). This follows from setting B = A in the tan(A + B) formula. It is undefined when tan^2 A = 1, i.e., A = pi/4 + n*pi/2.

title: Half Angle Formulas for Sine and Cosine
content: sin(A/2) = +/-sqrt((1 - cos A)/2) and cos(A/2) = +/-sqrt((1 + cos A)/2). The sign depends on the quadrant in which A/2 lies. These are derived from the double-angle formulas for cosine by replacing A with A/2.

title: Half Angle Formula for Tangent
content: tan(A/2) = sin A / (1 + cos A) = (1 - cos A) / sin A. An alternate form uses the substitution t = tan(A/2), giving sin A = 2t/(1+t^2) and cos A = (1-t^2)/(1+t^2). This t-substitution (Weierstrass substitution) is powerful for integration and solving equations.

title: Product-to-Sum Formulas
content: These convert products of sines and cosines into sums: 2 sin A cos B = sin(A+B) + sin(A-B), 2 cos A cos B = cos(A-B) + cos(A+B), 2 sin A sin B = cos(A-B) - cos(A+B). They are derived directly from the compound angle formulas by addition and subtraction.

title: Sum-to-Product Formulas
content: These convert sums/differences of sines and cosines into products: sin C + sin D = 2 sin((C+D)/2) cos((C-D)/2), sin C - sin D = 2 cos((C+D)/2) sin((C-D)/2), cos C + cos D = 2 cos((C+D)/2) cos((C-D)/2), cos C - cos D = -2 sin((C+D)/2) sin((C-D)/2). These are especially useful for solving trigonometric equations.

title: General Solution for sin(x) = sin(alpha)
content: The general solution of sin(x) = sin(alpha) is x = n*pi + (-1)^n * alpha, where n is any integer. This captures both the principal solution and all co-terminal and supplementary angle solutions on the sine curve.
example: Solve sin(x) = 1/2. Here alpha = pi/6. General solution: x = n*pi + (-1)^n * pi/6. For n=0: x=pi/6, n=1: x=5pi/6, n=2: x=13pi/6, etc.

title: General Solution for cos(x) = cos(alpha)
content: The general solution of cos(x) = cos(alpha) is x = 2n*pi +/- alpha, where n is any integer. This accounts for the even symmetry of the cosine function.
example: Solve cos(x) = 1/2. Here alpha = pi/3. General solution: x = 2n*pi +/- pi/3. This gives x = ..., -5pi/3, -pi/3, pi/3, 5pi/3, 7pi/3, ...

title: General Solution for tan(x) = tan(alpha)
content: The general solution of tan(x) = tan(alpha) is x = n*pi + alpha, where n is any integer. The tangent function has period pi and no symmetry ambiguity, making this the simplest general solution form.
example: Solve tan(x) = 1. Here alpha = pi/4. General solution: x = n*pi + pi/4, giving x = ..., -3pi/4, pi/4, 5pi/4, 9pi/4, ...

title: Equations Reducible to Quadratics
content: Many trigonometric equations can be converted into quadratic form using substitution (e.g., let t = sin x or t = cos x) or by using identities like cos(2x) = 1 - 2sin^2(x). After solving for t, back-substitute and apply the appropriate general solution formula. Always check that solutions satisfy the original domain constraints.
formula_latex: a\sin^2 x + b\sin x + c = 0 \implies \text{let } t = \sin x
example: Solve 2sin^2(x) - 3sin(x) + 1 = 0. Let t = sin(x): 2t^2 - 3t + 1 = 0 gives (2t-1)(t-1) = 0, so t = 1/2 or t = 1. Thus x = n*pi + (-1)^n*(pi/6) or x = (4n+1)*pi/2.

title: Domain and Range of Inverse Trigonometric Functions
content: The principal value branches are: arcsin: [-1,1] -> [-pi/2, pi/2], arccos: [-1,1] -> [0, pi], arctan: R -> (-pi/2, pi/2), arccot: R -> (0, pi), arcsec: |x|>=1 -> [0, pi]\{pi/2}, arccsc: |x|>=1 -> [-pi/2, pi/2]\{0}. These restricted ranges ensure the inverse functions are well-defined (one-to-one).
example: arcsin(1/2) = pi/6 (not 5pi/6, which is outside [-pi/2, pi/2]). arccos(-1/2) = 2pi/3 (not -2pi/3, which is outside [0, pi]).

title: Complementary Inverse Trigonometric Identities
content: arcsin(x) + arccos(x) = pi/2 for all x in [-1,1]. Similarly, arctan(x) + arccot(x) = pi/2 for all real x, and arcsec(x) + arccsc(x) = pi/2 for |x| >= 1. These reflect the co-function relationships sin and cos, tan and cot, sec and csc.
example: If arcsin(3/5) = alpha, then arccos(3/5) = pi/2 - alpha. Numerically, arcsin(0.6) approx 0.6435 and arccos(0.6) approx 0.9273, and their sum is pi/2 approx 1.5708.

title: Addition Formulas for Inverse Tangent
content: arctan(a) + arctan(b) = arctan((a+b)/(1-ab)) when ab < 1, arctan(a) + arctan(b) = pi + arctan((a+b)/(1-ab)) when ab > 1 and a > 0, and arctan(a) + arctan(b) = -pi + arctan((a+b)/(1-ab)) when ab > 1 and a < 0. This is crucial in JEE for simplifying sums of inverse tangents.

title: Inverse Trigonometric Functions of Negative Arguments
content: arcsin(-x) = -arcsin(x) (odd function), arccos(-x) = pi - arccos(x), arctan(-x) = -arctan(x) (odd function), arccot(-x) = pi - arccot(x). These symmetry properties are essential for evaluating inverse trig at negative arguments.
example: arcsin(-sqrt(3)/2) = -arcsin(sqrt(3)/2) = -pi/3. arccos(-1/2) = pi - arccos(1/2) = pi - pi/3 = 2pi/3.

title: Sine Rule (Law of Sines)
content: In any triangle ABC with sides a, b, c opposite to angles A, B, C respectively: a/sin A = b/sin B = c/sin C = 2R, where R is the circumradius. This is used when two angles and a side are known, or two sides and a non-included angle are given (ambiguous case).
example: In triangle ABC, A = 30, B = 45, a = 10. Then b = a*sin(B)/sin(A) = 10*sin(45)/sin(30) = 10*(sqrt(2)/2)/(1/2) = 10*sqrt(2).

title: Cosine Rule (Law of Cosines)
content: In any triangle ABC: a^2 = b^2 + c^2 - 2bc*cos(A), and similarly for b^2 and c^2 by cyclic permutation. This generalizes the Pythagorean theorem (when A = 90, the formula reduces to a^2 = b^2 + c^2). It is used when three sides are known (SSS) or two sides and the included angle (SAS).

title: Area of a Triangle Using Trigonometry
content: The area of triangle ABC = (1/2)*a*b*sin(C) = (1/2)*b*c*sin(A) = (1/2)*a*c*sin(B). This is derived from the standard base-height formula by expressing the height in terms of a sine. It is especially useful when two sides and the included angle are known.
example: In triangle with sides a = 8, b = 6, and included angle C = 30. Area = (1/2)(8)(6)sin(30) = 24*(1/2) = 12 square units.

title: Heights and Distances: Angle of Elevation and Depression
content: The angle of elevation is the angle between the horizontal and the line of sight looking upward at an object. The angle of depression is the angle below the horizontal when looking downward. By alternate interior angles, the angle of depression from point A to point B equals the angle of elevation from B to A. These problems typically use tan(theta) = opposite/adjacent in right triangles.
example: A tower is observed from a point 100m away. The angle of elevation to the top is 60. Height of tower = 100*tan(60) = 100*sqrt(3) approx 173.2 m.

title: Maximum and Minimum of a*sin(x) + b*cos(x)
content: The expression a*sin(x) + b*cos(x) can be written as R*sin(x + phi) where R = sqrt(a^2 + b^2) and tan(phi) = b/a. Therefore its maximum value is sqrt(a^2 + b^2) and minimum value is -sqrt(a^2 + b^2). This technique is frequently tested in JEE.
example: Find the maximum of 3sin(x) + 4cos(x). R = sqrt(9+16) = 5. Maximum value = 5, achieved when sin(x + phi) = 1 where phi = arctan(4/3).

title: Triple Angle Formulas
content: sin(3A) = 3sin(A) - 4sin^3(A) and cos(3A) = 4cos^3(A) - 3cos(A). Also tan(3A) = (3tan(A) - tan^3(A))/(1 - 3tan^2(A)). These are derived by writing 3A = 2A + A and applying compound and double angle formulas. They appear frequently in JEE Advanced problems.
